title: Basic Structure
lead: Based on the theory of ==implied limitation== — a constitutional law doctrine that places restrictions on amending certain essential or basic principles embedded in the Constitution.

items: Influenced by radical constitutional amendments in ==Weimar Germany== — German law professor ==Conrad== is credited with the genesis of the doctrine
Cemented in the landmark ==13-judge== bench of [[Kesavananda Bharati (1973)]] — ==Nani Palkhivala== credited with persuading the SC to formulate it
Context: land reforms, nationalisation and harmonising FRs with DPSPs — given effect through amendments affecting FRs, limiting Parliament's power to amend

blocks.text: A north star which guides and gives a certain direction to the interpreters and implementers of the Constitution when the path ahead is convoluted.
blocks.by: CJI Dr D. Y. Chandrachud, on the Basic Structure Doctrine

items.title: Checks & Balances
bullets: If Parliament had an unfettered right to amend, the SC has a ==coextensive power== to review and invalidate any amendment violating the basic structure.

items.title: Constitutionalism
bullets: Parliament holds constituent power ==in trust for the people== — it can never change the fundamental bases of India.

items.title: Domino Effect
bullets: Credited with protecting India from collapsing like its ==South Asian counterparts== — totalitarian rule, military coup, extra-constitutional means.

items.title: Judicial Review & Constitutional Supremacy
bullets: Reinforces judicial review; ensures ==supremacy of the Constitution== and prevents arbitrary legislative power.

items.title: Fundamental Rights
bullets: No amendment can ==dilute or abrogate== rights such as equality, free speech, protection against discrimination.

items.title: Maintaining Federalism
bullets: Prevents altering the ==Union–State distribution of powers==; keeps the federal balance.

items.title: Limiting Amendments
bullets: Sets a boundary: amendments cannot ==fundamentally transform== the Constitution's character.

items.title: Evolution of the Constitution
bullets: Allows ==progressive interpretation== — expanding rights and inclusivity — while the core stays protected.

items.title: Critique
bullets: Alleged danger to democracy (unelected judges veto elected majorities)

blocks.text: The Basic Structure is not a barrier to progress; it is a bridge between the past and the future — between what India aspired to be in 1950, and what it must never stop striving to become. Laws may change, but principles must endure — the soul of the Constitution remains inviolable, even as its body adapts to the needs of an evolving nation.
